segments.title: What makes someone a historian?
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke argues that sound research, source standards, and willingness to share historical understanding matter more than a formal academic qualification.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats historical practice rather than institutional status as the core test of a historian. He stresses research standards, careful use of sources, and communicating what historical study has revealed. Academic training can be valuable because it exposes researchers to sustained challenge, citation, and the need to defend a case, but he does not present a qualification as a prerequisite for serious historical work. Drawing on naval and military history, he notes that non-academic historians often provide rigorous and welcome criticism at conferences, while personal attacks remain outside legitimate debate. Qualifications are therefore most necessary for someone who wants a university teaching career, not for everyone who wishes to study and explain history responsibly.
segments.question: Does someone need academic qualifications to be a historian, and what distinguishes academic from non-academic historians?
segments.answerShort: No. He argues that historians are defined chiefly by rigorous research, responsible source use, and sharing historical understanding. Academic credentials mainly matter for university employment and can provide training in adversarial scholarly debate, while serious non-academic historians can apply the same standards.

evidence.note: He contrasts qualifications with research and source standards, explains academic debate, and uses naval and military history to describe rigorous non-academic participation.
